background: Children out of School in America is a report on delinquency of school-age children prepared by the Children's Defense Fund (CDF), a government-sponsored organization. The report includes three reported results based on surveys conducted in specific regions. The details of the reported results and the corresponding facts are provided.

task.query: Analyze Reported result 1: $25 \%$ of the 16- and 17-year-olds in the Portland, Maine, Bayside East Housing Project were out of school. Fact: Only eight children were surveyed; two were found to be out of school. Identify the potential distortions in this result.
task.answer: The reporting of $25 \%$ is misleading because it is based on a very small sample size of only eight children. Reporting percentages instead of the actual numbers (2 out of 8) can lead to misinterpretation of the data. The small sample size makes it unreliable to draw any meaningful inferences.

task.query: Analyze Reported result 2: Of all the secondary-school students who had been suspended more than once in census tract 22 in Columbia, South Carolina, $33 \%$ had been suspended two times and $67 \%$ had been suspended three or more times. Fact: CDF found only three children in that entire census tract who had been suspended; one child was suspended twice and the other two children, three or more times. Identify the potential distortions in this result.
task.answer: The reporting of $33 \%$ and $67 \%$ is misleading because it is based on an extremely small sample size of only three children. Using percentages instead of the actual numbers (1 child suspended twice and 2 children suspended three or more times) can exaggerate the significance of the data. The small sample size makes the results unreliable for generalization.

task.query: Analyze Reported result 3: In the Portland Bayside East Housing Project, $50 \%$ of all the secondary-school children who had been suspended more than once had been suspended three or more times. Fact: The survey found two secondary-school children had been suspended in that area; one of them had been suspended three or more times. Identify the potential distortions in this result.
task.answer: The reporting of $50 \%$ is misleading because it is based on a very small sample size of only two children. Reporting percentages instead of the actual numbers (1 out of 2) can lead to misinterpretation of the data. The small sample size makes it unreliable to draw any meaningful inferences.

task.query: Summarize the overall issue with the results reported by the CDF in these examples.
task.answer: In each of these examples, the reporting of percentages (i.e., relative frequencies) instead of the numbers themselves is misleading. No inference we might draw from the cited examples would be reliable. In short, either the report should state the numbers alone instead of percentages, or, better yet, it should state that the numbers were too small to report by region.

meta_info.caption: More Misleading Descriptive Statistics- Delinquent Children
meta_info.chapter: Methods for Describing Sets of Data
meta_info.section: 2.10 Distorting the Truth with Descriptive Techniques
